parts.content: Calculate the efficiency of the heat engine.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the efficiency formula: $\text{Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Work output}}{\text{Heat input}} = \frac{Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$

- Substitute values and calculate: $\text{Efficiency} = \frac{1000-600}{1000}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State the final answer: $0.4$ or $40\%$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify known variables
steps.content:
To find the efficiency of the heat engine, we first need to identify the energy entering and leaving the system. From the problem description, we know the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir ($Q_{\text{in}}$) and the heat rejected to the cold reservoir ($Q_{\text{out}}$).

- Heat absorbed ($Q_{\text{in}}$) = $1000\text{ J}$
- Heat rejected ($Q_{\text{out}}$) = $600\text{ J}$

steps.title: Calculate useful work output
steps.content:
In a heat engine, the useful work output ($W$) is the difference between the heat energy taken in and the heat energy released. This is based on the principle of conservation of energy covered in **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**.

$$\begin{aligned} W &= Q_{\text{in}} - Q_{\text{out}} \\ W &= 1000\text{ J} - 600\text{ J} \\ W &= 400\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the two values on the main calculation screen.

ti84.instructions: Enter the subtraction directly on the home screen.

calculator.description: Calculate the work output by subtracting the heat rejected from the heat absorbed.

steps.title: Calculate the efficiency
steps.content:
Efficiency ($\eta$) is defined as the ratio of useful work output to the total heat energy input. 

$$\text{Efficiency} = \frac{\text{Useful Work Output}}{\text{Total Heat Input}}$$

Substituting our values into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{W}{Q_{\text{in}}} \\ \eta &= \frac{400}{1000} \end{aligned}$$

This substitution earns you the first mark (**M1**).

gdc.instructions: Calculate the ratio of work to heat input.

ti84.instructions: Divide the previous result (400) by the input heat (1000).

calculator.description: Divide the work output by the heat input to find the efficiency.

steps.title: State final answer
steps.content:
Perform the final calculation to find the efficiency as a decimal or a percentage:

$$\eta = 0.4$$

To express this as a percentage, multiply by $100$, giving $40\%$. This final result earns the second mark (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content:
From the question, we are given the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir ($Q_{in}$) and the heat rejected to the cold reservoir ($Q_{out}$):

- $Q_{in} = 1000$ J
- $Q_{out} = 600$ J

This information is essential for calculating the efficiency using the heat ratio method.

steps.title: Apply the efficiency formula
steps.content:
The efficiency ($\eta$) of a heat engine can be expressed by the ratio of the heat output to the heat input. Using the fundamental definition of efficiency from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, we use the formula:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$$

This formula allows us to see how much of the energy absorbed is *not* converted into work.

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the equation to find the efficiency. This step is where you earn the first mark (**M1**) in the markscheme:

$$\begin{aligned} \eta &= 1 - \frac{600}{1000} \\ \eta &= 1 - 0.6 \\ \eta &= 0.4 \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type 1 - (600 / 1000) into the main calculation window.

ti84.instructions: Enter 1 - (600 / 1000) on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the efficiency of the heat engine.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The final efficiency of the heat engine is $0.4$, or $40\%$. 

In IB Physics, efficiency is usually expressed as a decimal or a percentage. Ensure your final answer matches the accuracy of the provided values (**A1**).

steps.title: Identify reservoir temperatures
steps.content:
To calculate the efficiency using the temperature-based approach, we first identify the absolute temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs given in the problem.

- Temperature of the hot reservoir ($T_{hot}$) = $500 \text{ K}$
- Temperature of the cold reservoir ($T_{cold}$) = $300 \text{ K}$

steps.title: Apply efficiency formula
steps.content:
In thermodynamics, specifically when dealing with ideal heat engines (Carnot engines), the efficiency ($\eta$) can be determined using the absolute temperatures of the reservoirs. This is covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics**.

The formula is:
$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}}$$

steps.title: Substitute and calculate
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the given temperature values into the formula to find the efficiency:

$$\begin{aligned} \eta &= 1 - \frac{300}{500} \\ \eta &= 1 - 0.6 \\ \eta &= 0.4 \end{aligned}$$

To express this as a percentage, we multiply by $100$, giving $40\%$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expression '1 - (300 / 500)' in the calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter '1 - (300 / 500)' on the home screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the efficiency using reservoir temperatures.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The efficiency of the heat engine is **0.4** (or **40%**). 

Note: In this specific problem, calculating efficiency using temperatures yields the same result as using heat values ($Q$), indicating this engine is operating at its maximum theoretical (Carnot) efficiency.

parts.content: Compare the efficiency of this engine to that of a Carnot engine operating between the same temperatures.
parts.markscheme:
- State the efficiency of a Carnot engine: $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$

- Substitute values and calculate Carnot efficiency: $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{300}{500} = 0.4$ or $40\%$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Compare the efficiencies: Both the Carnot engine and this heat engine have an efficiency of $40\%$, indicating that this engine operates at maximum theoretical efficiency for these temperatures (it is a reversible engine). <marks>R1</marks>

<note>Award marks for calculation and valid comparison/conclusion.</note>

2 marks total

steps.title: Calculate real engine efficiency
steps.content:
First, we calculate the efficiency ($\eta$) of the given heat engine. Efficiency is the ratio of the useful work done to the energy absorbed. Using the values for heat absorbed ($Q_{\text{in}}$) and heat rejected ($Q_{\text{out}}$), the formula is:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_{\text{out}}}{Q_{\text{in}}}$$

Substituting the values from the question:
$$\eta = 1 - \frac{600}{1000} = 0.4$$

This gives an efficiency of $40\%$.

calculator.description: Calculate the decimal efficiency of the engine.

steps.title: Calculate Carnot efficiency
steps.content:
The Carnot efficiency represents the maximum theoretical efficiency any engine can achieve between two specific temperatures. It is calculated using the absolute temperatures of the hot reservoir ($T_{\text{hot}}$) and cold reservoir ($T_{\text{cold}}$):

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$$

Substituting the temperatures provided:
$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{300}{500} = 0.4$$

This results in a maximum theoretical efficiency of $40\%$.

calculator.description: Calculate the Carnot efficiency.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Now we compare the two values:

- Real engine efficiency: $40\%$
- Carnot engine efficiency: $40\%$

Since the efficiency of this engine is equal to the Carnot efficiency, it operates at the maximum possible theoretical efficiency. This indicates that the engine is a reversible engine.

steps.title: Identify given values
steps.content:
First, we identify the temperatures of the reservoirs and the heat energy transferred during one cycle:

*   Hot reservoir temperature ($T_h$): $500 \text{ K}$
*   Cold reservoir temperature ($T_c$): $300 \text{ K}$
*   Heat absorbed ($Q_h$): $1000 \text{ J}$
*   Heat rejected ($Q_c$): $600 \text{ J}$

steps.title: Calculate the temperature ratio
steps.content:
The maximum theoretical efficiency (Carnot efficiency) is determined by the ratio of the absolute temperatures of the reservoirs. Let's calculate the ratio $\frac{T_c}{T_h}$:

$$\frac{T_c}{T_h} = \frac{300 \text{ K}}{500 \text{ K}} = 0.6$$

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the absolute temperatures.

steps.title: Calculate the heat ratio
steps.content:
Now, we calculate the actual ratio of heat rejected to heat absorbed for this specific engine:

$$\frac{Q_c}{Q_h} = \frac{600 \text{ J}}{1000 \text{ J}} = 0.6$$

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the heat energy rejected to absorbed.

steps.title: Compare efficiencies
steps.content:
A reversible (Carnot) engine satisfies the condition $\frac{Q_c}{Q_h} = \frac{T_c}{T_h}$. 

Since both ratios are equal to $0.6$, the efficiency of this engine is exactly equal to the Carnot efficiency:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{Q_c}{Q_h} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 \text{ (or } 40\%\text{)}$$

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h} = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4 \text{ (or } 40\%\text{)}$$

Because the ratios match, the engine operates at the maximum theoretical efficiency for these temperatures.

calculator.description: Calculate the final efficiency percentage.

steps.title: Identify the comparison method
steps.content: To compare the efficiencies, we will use the **Work Output Comparison** approach. This involves calculating how much work our engine actually does and comparing it to the maximum theoretical work a Carnot engine would do using the same amount of input heat and the same reservoirs.

steps.title: Calculate actual work output
steps.content:
First, we find the actual work done ($W_{actual}$) by the engine during one cycle. From the first law of thermodynamics, work is the difference between the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir ($Q_{in}$) and the heat rejected to the cold reservoir ($Q_{out}$).

$$\begin{aligned} W_{actual} &= Q_{in} - Q_{out} \\ W_{actual} &= 1000\text{ J} - 600\text{ J} \\ W_{actual} &= 400\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the heat out from the heat in.

ti84.instructions: Enter the subtraction directly on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the expression into a new line.

calculator.description: Calculate the actual work output.

steps.title: Find Carnot efficiency
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the efficiency of a Carnot engine ($\eta_{\text{Carnot}}$) operating between the same temperature limits. These concepts are covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics (HL only)**. 

Using the formula from the markscheme:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_{\text{cold}}}{T_{\text{hot}}}$$

Substituting the temperatures (in Kelvin):

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{300}{500} = 0.4$$

This means the maximum theoretical efficiency is $40\%$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the temperature ratio and subtract from 1.

ti84.instructions: Use the division and subtraction operators.

calculator.description: Calculate Carnot efficiency.

steps.title: Calculate theoretical Carnot work
steps.content:
Now, let's see how much work a Carnot engine would produce ($W_{\text{Carnot}}$) if it absorbed the same $1000\text{ J}$ of heat:

$$\begin{aligned} W_{\text{Carnot}} &= \eta_{\text{Carnot}} \times Q_{in} \\ W_{\text{Carnot}} &= 0.4 \times 1000\text{ J} \\ W_{\text{Carnot}} &= 400\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

ti84.instructions: Multiply the efficiency by the input heat.

calculator.description: Calculate theoretical maximum work.

steps.title: Compare and conclude
steps.content:
Finally, we compare the two values. 

Since the actual work output ($400\text{ J}$) is exactly equal to the theoretical maximum work output ($400\text{ J}$), the efficiency of this engine is identical to that of a Carnot engine.

$$\eta_{\text{engine}} = \eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 40\%$$

This indicates that the engine is a **reversible** heat engine operating at the maximum possible theoretical efficiency for these temperatures.

parts.content: Explain how entropy changes for the engine and the reservoirs in this cycle, and discuss whether the second law of thermodynamics is upheld.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculate the entropy change for the hot and cold reservoirs: $\Delta S_{\text{hot}} = -\frac{1000}{500} = -2 \, \text{J K}^{-1}$ and $\Delta S_{\text{cold}} = \frac{600}{300} = +2 \, \text{J K}^{-1}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- State that the entropy change of the engine is zero because it operates in a cycle: $\Delta S_{\text{engine}} = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Conclude that the total entropy change is zero, satisfying the second law of thermodynamics (reversible process): $\Delta S_{\text{total}} = -2 + 2 + 0 = 0$ <marks>R1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Calculate hot reservoir entropy change
steps.content:
To find the entropy change ($ΔS$) of the hot reservoir, we use the fundamental definition of entropy change for a constant temperature process:

$$\Delta S = \frac{Q}{T}$$

Since the hot reservoir **absorbs 1000 J** from its perspective (meaning 1000 J leaves the reservoir), $Q$ is negative for the reservoir. 

$$\Delta S_{\text{hot}} = \frac{-1000 \text{ J}}{500 \text{ K}} = -2.0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

highlights.text: absorbs 1000 J of heat from the hot reservoir

steps.title: Calculate cold reservoir entropy change
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the entropy change for the cold reservoir. In this case, the engine **rejects 600 J** to it, so heat enters the cold reservoir ($Q$ is positive).

$$\Delta S_{\text{cold}} = \frac{+600 \text{ J}}{300 \text{ K}} = +2.0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

highlights.text: rejects 600 J of heat to the cold reservoir

steps.title: Identify engine entropy change
steps.content:
Entropy is a **state function**, which means its value depends only on the current state of the system, not the path taken. Because the engine operates in a complete cycle, it returns exactly to its initial state at the end of the process.

Therefore, the entropy change for the engine itself is:

$$\Delta S_{\text{engine}} = 0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Sum total entropy change
steps.content:
The total entropy change of the universe ($\Delta S_{\text{total}}$) is the sum of the changes for all components involved:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta S_{\text{total}} &= \Delta S_{\text{hot}} + \Delta S_{\text{cold}} + \Delta S_{\text{engine}} \\ \Delta S_{\text{total}} &= -2.0 + 2.0 + 0 \\ \Delta S_{\text{total}} &= 0 \text{ J K}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: In the main calculation menu, type -2 + 2 + 0 and press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Enter -2 + 2 + 0 on the main screen and press ENTER.

calculator.description: Calculate the total entropy change by summing the individual components.

steps.title: Evaluate Second Law compliance
steps.content:
The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** states that the total entropy of an isolated system (the universe) must either increase or remain constant ($\Delta S_{\text{total}} \geq 0$).

Since we calculated $\Delta S_{\text{total}} = 0$, the Second Law is upheld. A total entropy change of zero indicates that this specific cycle is **reversible** (a Carnot cycle).

steps.title: Analyze efficiency and reversibility
steps.content:
To understand the cycle, we first compare the actual efficiency ($\eta_{act}$) of the engine to the maximum theoretical (Carnot) efficiency ($\eta_{C}$).

The actual efficiency is calculated from the heat values:
$$\eta_{act} = 1 - \frac{Q_{cold}}{Q_{hot}} = 1 - \frac{600}{1000} = 0.4$$

The Carnot efficiency is determined by the reservoir temperatures:
$$\eta_{C} = 1 - \frac{T_{cold}}{T_{hot}} = 1 - \frac{300}{500} = 0.4$$

Since the actual efficiency equals the Carnot efficiency ($\eta_{act} = \eta_{C}$), this engine operates as a **reversible** heat engine. This is a crucial finding for calculating entropy changes.

calculator.description: Calculate actual and Carnot efficiencies to check for reversibility.

steps.title: Calculate reservoir entropy changes
steps.content:
Entropy change is defined as $\Delta S = \frac{Q}{T}$ (for constant temperature). We apply this to both reservoirs:

1. **Hot Reservoir:** It loses heat ($Q = -1000 \text{ J}$):
$$\Delta S_{hot} = \frac{-1000 \text{ J}}{500 \text{ K}} = -2.0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

2. **Cold Reservoir:** It gains heat ($Q = +600 \text{ J}$):
$$\Delta S_{cold} = \frac{+600 \text{ J}}{300 \text{ K}} = +2.0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate entropy changes for both reservoirs.

steps.title: Evaluate engine and total entropy
steps.content:
Because the engine operates in a cycle, it returns to its exact initial state at the end of each process. Since entropy is a **state function**, its change over a complete cycle must be zero:
$$\Delta S_{engine} = 0$$

Now, we sum the total entropy change of the universe (engine + reservoirs):
$$\Delta S_{total} = \Delta S_{hot} + \Delta S_{cold} + \Delta S_{engine}$$
$$\Delta S_{total} = -2.0 \text{ J K}^{-1} + 2.0 \text{ J K}^{-1} + 0 = 0$$

A total entropy change of zero indicates a **reversible process**, which upholds the **Second Law of Thermodynamics** (which states that $\Delta S_{total} \ge 0$ for any process).

calculator.description: Sum the entropy changes to find the total change.

parts.content: Calculate the maximum possible efficiency of this engine.
parts.markscheme: $\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 1 - \frac{300}{500} = 0.40$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the Carnot Cycle
steps.content:
The question describes an engine operating with a cyclic process consisting of **two adiabatic and two isothermal steps**. This specific sequence defines a **Carnot cycle**. 

The maximum possible efficiency for any heat engine operating between two temperatures is given by the Carnot efficiency, which depends only on the absolute temperatures of the hot ($T_H$) and cold ($T_C$) reservoirs. This concept is covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics (HL only)**.

highlights.text: two adiabatic and two isothermal steps

steps.title: Extract Reservoir Temperatures
steps.content:
To calculate the efficiency, we need the absolute temperatures (in Kelvin) of the two reservoirs. From the problem specification:

- Temperature of the hot reservoir: $T_H = 500\ \text{K}$
- Temperature of the cold reservoir: $T_C = 300\ \text{K}$

steps.title: Apply Carnot Efficiency Formula
steps.content:
Using the Direct Temperature Ratio approach, we substitute the values into the Carnot efficiency formula:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - \frac{300}{500}$$

gdc.instructions: Type 1 - 300/500 into the calculation screen and press EXE.

calculator.description: Calculate the efficiency using the temperature ratio.

steps.title: State Final Efficiency
steps.content:
Performing the calculation:

$$\eta_{\text{Carnot}} = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40$$

The maximum possible efficiency of this engine is **0.40** (or 40%). 

In an IB exam, you would earn **1 mark** (A1) for correctly applying the formula and reaching this final value.

steps.title: Analyze the reversible cycle
steps.content:
For a reversible engine, such as a Carnot cycle, the heat exchanged with a reservoir is directly proportional to its absolute temperature. We can express this relationship as:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

Where:
- $Q_H$ is the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir ($2000\text{ J}$).
- $T_H$ is the hot reservoir temperature ($500\text{ K}$).
- $Q_C$ is the heat rejected to the cold reservoir.
- $T_C$ is the cold reservoir temperature ($300\text{ K}$).

highlights.text: thermal energy from a hot reservoir at $T_H = 500\text{ K}$

steps.title: Calculate heat rejected ($Q_C$)
steps.content:
Rearranging the proportionality equation, we can solve for the heat rejected to the cold reservoir ($Q_C$):

$$\begin{aligned} Q_C &= Q_H \cdot \frac{T_C}{T_H} \\ Q_C &= 2000 \cdot \frac{300}{500} \\ Q_C &= 1200\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Multiply the heat absorbed by the temperature ratio.

ti84.instructions: Multiply the heat absorbed by the temperature ratio.

desmos.instructions: Multiply the heat absorbed by the temperature ratio.

calculator.description: Calculate the heat rejected $Q_C$.

steps.title: Determine net work done
steps.content:
Using the First Law of Thermodynamics for a complete cycle, the net work done ($W$) is the difference between the heat absorbed and the heat rejected:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= Q_H - Q_C \\ W &= 2000 - 1200 \\ W &= 800\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the calculated heat rejected from the initial heat absorbed.

ti84.instructions: Subtract the calculated heat rejected from the initial heat absorbed.

desmos.instructions: Subtract the calculated heat rejected from the initial heat absorbed.

calculator.description: Subtract rejected heat from absorbed heat to find work.

steps.title: Calculate thermal efficiency
steps.content:
The efficiency ($\eta$) is defined as the ratio of the useful work output to the total heat input:

$$\begin{aligned} \eta &= \frac{W}{Q_H} \\ \eta &= \frac{800}{2000} \\ \eta &= 0.40 \end{aligned}$$

This confirms the maximum possible efficiency for this engine is $40\%$. Note that this matches the standard Carnot efficiency formula $\eta = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$ used in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics**.

gdc.instructions: Divide the calculated work by the total heat absorbed.

ti84.instructions: Divide the calculated work by the total heat absorbed.

desmos.instructions: Divide the calculated work by the total heat absorbed.

calculator.description: Divide net work by initial heat input.

parts.content: Determine the maximum useful work that the engine can perform per cycle.
parts.markscheme:
Use of $W = \eta Q_H$ or substitution $W = 0.40 \times 2000$ <marks>M1</marks>
$800\ \text{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
To find the maximum useful work, we first need to extract the relevant values from the question. The engine operates between two temperatures and absorbs a specific amount of heat energy.

*   Temperature of the hot reservoir: $T_H = 500\text{ K}$
*   Temperature of the cold reservoir: $T_C = 300\text{ K}$
*   Thermal energy absorbed: $Q_H = 2000\text{ J}$

steps.title: Calculate Carnot efficiency
steps.content:
Since this engine follows a Carnot-like cycle, its maximum possible efficiency (the Carnot efficiency, $\eta_C$) depends only on the temperatures of the reservoirs. This is covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics**.

The formula for Carnot efficiency is:
$$\eta_C = 1 - \frac{T_C}{T_H}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the efficiency of the cycle using the given temperatures.

steps.title: Evaluate the efficiency
steps.content:
Substituting the values into the formula:

$$\begin{aligned} \eta_C &= 1 - \frac{300}{500} \\ \eta_C &= 1 - 0.60 \\ \eta_C &= 0.40 \end{aligned}$$

This means the engine converts $40\%$ of the absorbed heat into useful work.

steps.title: Calculate maximum useful work
steps.content:
Now we use the general definition of efficiency from **Topic A.3: Work, energy and power**, which relates work done ($W$) to the energy input ($Q_H$):

$$\eta = \frac{W}{Q_H}$$

Rearranging for $W$:

$$W = \eta \times Q_H$$

calculator.description: Multiply the efficiency by the heat absorbed to find the work done.

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
By substituting our values, we find:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= 0.40 \times 2000 \\ W &= 800\text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

The maximum useful work the engine can perform per cycle is $800\text{ J}$.

**Marking Note:** In the IB exam, one mark is awarded for the correct substitution into the efficiency-work relationship, and the second mark is for the correct final answer with units.

steps.title: Identify Carnot cycle relationship
steps.content:
In a Carnot cycle (consisting of two isothermal and two adiabatic processes), the ratio of the thermal energy transferred to the absolute temperature of the reservoir is constant. This relationship is covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics**.

We can relate the heat absorbed from the hot reservoir ($Q_H$) and the heat rejected to the cold reservoir ($Q_C$) using their respective temperatures:

$$\frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{Q_C}{T_C}$$

steps.title: Calculate heat rejected
steps.content:
Using the values provided in the question, we can solve for the thermal energy rejected to the cold reservoir ($Q_C$):

$$\begin{aligned} Q_C &= Q_H \times \frac{T_C}{T_H} \\ Q_C &= 2000 \times \frac{300}{500} \\ Q_C &= 1200 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This is the amount of energy that cannot be converted into work and must be exhausted from the system.

gdc.instructions: Multiply 2000 by the fraction 300 divided by 500.

calculator.description: Calculate the heat rejected to the cold reservoir.

steps.title: Calculate maximum useful work
steps.content:
The First Law of Thermodynamics for a complete cycle tells us that the net work done ($W$) is the difference between the energy absorbed and the energy rejected (the energy balance):

$$\begin{aligned} W &= Q_H - Q_C \\ W &= 2000 \text{ J} - 1200 \text{ J} \\ W &= 800 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

This represents the maximum work possible for an engine operating between these two temperatures.

calculator.description: Calculate the net work by finding the difference between heat in and heat out.

parts.content: Calculate the entropy change of the gas over one complete cycle.
parts.markscheme:
For a cyclic process, total change in entropy of the system = 0 <marks>A1</marks>
Because the gas returns to its original state after each cycle <marks>R1</marks>

steps.title: Identify entropy as a state function
steps.content:
This question asks for the entropy change over a **complete cycle**. To solve this efficiently, we use the **State Function Principle**.

Entropy ($S$) is a **state function**, which means its value depends only on the current state of the system (defined by variables like pressure, volume, and temperature), not on how the system reached that state.

steps.title: Analyze the cyclic process
steps.content:
The problem states that the engine operates with a **cyclic process**. In a cyclic process, the system starts at an initial state and performs a series of transformations (expansions and compressions) before returning to the exact same initial conditions ($P, V, T$).

Because the system returns to its original state, the final value of any state function must equal its initial value.

steps.title: Calculate the change
steps.content:
Using the definition of a change in a state function:

$$\Delta S_{\text{gas}} = S_{\text{final}} - S_{\text{initial}}$$ 

Since the final state is identical to the initial state ($S_{\text{final}} = S_{\text{initial}}$):

$$\Delta S_{\text{gas}} = 0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The total change in entropy of the gas over one complete cycle is **zero**.

This applies to any state function (like internal energy or entropy) in a closed loop cycle.

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content:
To find the total entropy change over one complete cycle, we will calculate the entropy change $\Delta S$ for each of the four steps (expansion, compression, and two adiabatic processes) and sum them up.

The general definition for the change in entropy during a reversible process at constant temperature is:

$$\Delta S = \frac{Q}{T}$$

steps.title: Isothermal expansion
steps.content:
First, we calculate the entropy change during the isothermal expansion. The gas absorbs thermal energy $Q_H$ from the hot reservoir at temperature $T_H$.

Using the given values:
$$\Delta S_{\text{exp}} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H} = \frac{2000}{500}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the heat absorbed by the temperature

ti84.instructions: Divide the heat absorbed by the temperature

desmos.instructions: Divide the heat absorbed by the temperature

calculator.description: Calculate entropy change for expansion

steps.title: Result for expansion
steps.content: $$\Delta S_{\text{exp}} = 4.0\ \text{J K}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Adiabatic steps
steps.content:
Next, consider the two adiabatic steps. An **adiabatic** process is defined as one where no thermal energy is transferred to or from the gas ($Q = 0$).

Since $Q = 0$:
$$\Delta S_{\text{adiabatic}} = \frac{0}{T} = 0\ \text{J K}^{-1}$$

Both adiabatic expansion and adiabatic compression contribute $0$ change to the entropy.

steps.title: Isothermal compression
steps.content:
Now we find the entropy change for the isothermal compression at $T_C = 300\ \text{K}$. For a Carnot cycle, the relationship between heat and temperature is $\frac{Q_C}{T_C} = \frac{Q_H}{T_H}$.

Since the gas rejects heat, $Q$ is negative. The magnitude of heat rejected $Q_C$ is:
$$Q_C = Q_H \times \frac{T_C}{T_H} = 2000 \times \frac{300}{500} = 1200\ \text{J}$$

The entropy change for compression (where heat is lost) is:
$$\Delta S_{\text{comp}} = \frac{-Q_C}{T_C} = \frac{-1200}{300}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide the negative heat rejected by the cold temperature

ti84.instructions: Divide the negative heat rejected by the cold temperature

desmos.instructions: Divide the negative heat rejected by the cold temperature

calculator.description: Calculate entropy change for compression

steps.title: Result for compression
steps.content: $$\Delta S_{\text{comp}} = -4.0\ \text{J K}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Sum total entropy
steps.content:
Finally, we sum the entropy changes for all four steps in the cycle:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta S_{\text{total}} &= \Delta S_{\text{exp}} + \Delta S_{\text{adia1}} + \Delta S_{\text{comp}} + \Delta S_{\text{adia2}} \\ \Delta S_{\text{total}} &= 4.0 + 0 + (-4.0) + 0 \\ \Delta S_{\text{total}} &= 0\ \text{J K}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
The total entropy change of the gas over one complete cycle is **$0\ \text{J K}^{-1}$**.

This makes sense because entropy is a state function. Since the gas returns to its exact original state (pressure, volume, and temperature) at the end of the cycle, the net change in any state property must be zero.

parts.content: Explain why the total entropy change of the universe is greater than zero for a real engine, but zero for an ideal reversible engine.
parts.markscheme:
In an ideal Carnot engine, processes are reversible and no entropy is produced; entropy changes in hot and cold reservoirs cancel out <marks>A1</marks>
Real engines involve irreversible processes (e.g. friction, turbulence), leading to net increase in entropy of the universe <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the Ideal Engine
steps.content:
First, let's look at the **ideal reversible engine** (like the Carnot cycle mentioned). By definition, a reversible process is one where the system and surroundings can be returned to their original states with no net change in the universe.

In this ideal case, the entropy lost by the hot reservoir is exactly equal to the entropy gained by the cold reservoir. Because the process is perfectly efficient within the limits of the temperatures, these changes cancel out completely.

$$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = \Delta S_{\text{system}} + \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} = 0$$

steps.title: Analyze the Real Engine
steps.content:
Now consider a **real engine**. In the real world, processes are **irreversible** due to factors such as friction, turbulence, and imperfect thermal contact. 

These factors cause energy to dissipate into "useless" forms (like waste heat) that cannot be fully recovered. This dissipation increases the disorder of the surroundings more than the ideal transfer would alone.

steps.title: Conclusion on Entropy Change
steps.content:
The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** states that for any irreversible process, the total entropy of the universe must increase.

*   **Ideal:** No entropy is produced ($ΔS_{\text{universe}} = 0$).
*   **Real:** Irreversible factors like friction generate additional entropy, so the total entropy of the universe increases ($ΔS_{\text{universe}} > 0$).

steps.title: Define entropy change formula
steps.content:
To analyze the entropy change quantitatively, we use the definition of entropy change for a reservoir at constant temperature:

$$\Delta S = \frac{Q}{T}$$

We need to calculate the total change in entropy for the universe, which is the sum of the entropy changes for the hot reservoir ($T_H$) and the cold reservoir ($T_C$).

$$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = \Delta S_H + \Delta S_C$$

steps.title: Calculate ideal engine entropy
steps.content:
For an **ideal reversible engine** (Carnot), the efficiency is maximum. We can calculate the heat rejected ($Q_C$) using the Carnot relationship $\frac{Q_C}{Q_H} = \frac{T_C}{T_H}$:

$$\begin{aligned} Q_C &= Q_H \times \frac{T_C}{T_H} \\ &= 2000 \times \frac{300}{500} \\ &= 1200 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Now, calculate the total entropy change:
- Hot reservoir (loses heat): $$\Delta S_H = \frac{-2000}{500} = -4.0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$
- Cold reservoir (gains heat): $$\Delta S_C = \frac{+1200}{300} = +4.0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

**Total:** $$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = -4.0 + 4.0 = 0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Verify the ideal entropy balance

steps.title: Analyze real engine efficiency
steps.content:
In a **real engine**, irreversible processes like friction and turbulence reduce the efficiency. This means less work is done for the same input energy ($Q_H = 2000 \text{ J}$).

By conservation of energy ($Q_H = W + Q_{\text{real}}$), if work ($W$) decreases, the heat rejected to the cold reservoir ($Q_{\text{real}}$) must **increase** compared to the ideal case.

$$Q_{\text{real}} > 1200 \text{ J}$$

steps.title: Calculate real engine entropy
steps.content:
Let's assume the real engine rejects just a bit more heat, for example, $Q_{\text{real}} = 1400 \text{ J}$.

The entropy change of the hot reservoir remains the same (determined by input), but the cold reservoir gains more entropy:

- Hot reservoir: $$\Delta S_H = \frac{-2000}{500} = -4.0 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$
- Cold reservoir: $$\Delta S_C = \frac{+1400}{300} \approx +4.67 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

**Total:** $$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = -4.0 + 4.67 = +0.67 \text{ J K}^{-1}$$

This positive result confirms that for any real engine where $Q_{\text{real}} > Q_{\text{ideal}}$, the total entropy change is greater than zero.

calculator.description: Verify the real entropy imbalance

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
The calculations show that an ideal reversible engine has $\Delta S_{\text{universe}} = 0$ because the entropy changes cancel out exactly. 

However, a real engine involves **irreversible processes** (like friction) that result in more heat rejection, causing a net increase in the entropy of the universe ($$\Delta S_{\text{universe}} > 0$$).

parts.content: State the Kelvin–Planck formulation of the second law of thermodynamics and use it to explain why no heat engine can have an efficiency of $100\%$.
parts.markscheme:
State: No process is possible in which the sole result is the absorption of heat from a reservoir and its complete conversion into work. 
OR 
It is impossible to extract heat from a hot reservoir and convert it entirely into work in a cycle without rejecting some heat. <marks>A1</marks>

Explanation: For a heat engine to operate in a cycle, it must reject some thermal energy to a cold reservoir (at a lower temperature). <marks>A1</marks>

Since efficiency $\eta = \frac{W}{Q_{in}} = \frac{Q_{in} - Q_{out}}{Q_{in}}$ and $Q_{out}$ cannot be zero, $\eta$ must always be less than $1$ (or $100\%$). <marks>A1</marks>

parts.content: Derive the equation for work done during an adiabatic process, relating it to energy conservation.
parts.markscheme:
- State the first law of thermodynamics: $\Delta U = Q - W$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Identify that for an adiabatic process, $Q = 0$ <marks>M1</marks>
$$
\therefore \Delta U = -W
$$
- Relate this to the change in internal energy for a monoatomic ideal gas to find work done: $W = -\Delta U = -\frac{3}{2}nR\Delta T$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award the final A1 mark for the correct equation relating work done to the change in temperature.</note>

steps.title: State the First Law
steps.content:
We begin with the First Law of Thermodynamics, which represents the conservation of energy. It relates the change in internal energy ($\Delta U$) to the heat added to the system ($Q$) and the work done by the system ($W$):

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

steps.title: Apply adiabatic condition
steps.content:
The question specifies that the gas undergoes an **adiabatic process**. By definition, this means there is no transfer of heat into or out of the system.

$$Q = 0$$

steps.title: Relate work to internal energy
steps.content:
Substituting $Q = 0$ into the First Law equation gives us a direct relationship between the change in internal energy and the work done:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta U &= 0 - W \\ \Delta U &= -W \\ W &= -\Delta U \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Internal energy of monoatomic gas
steps.content:
For a monoatomic ideal gas, the internal energy is the total kinetic energy of the particles. We use the average kinetic energy formula from the data booklet:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

The total internal energy $U$ is the sum of the energy of all $N$ particles ($U = N \times \overline{E_{k}}$). Using the ideal gas relation $Nk_B = nR$:

$$U = N \left( \frac{3}{2} k_B T \right) = \frac{3}{2} (N k_B) T = \frac{3}{2} nRT$$

Therefore, the change in internal energy is:

$$\Delta U = \frac{3}{2}nR\Delta T$$

steps.title: Final derivation
steps.content:
Finally, we substitute the expression for $\Delta U$ back into our work equation ($W = -\Delta U$) to get the final answer:

$$W = -\frac{3}{2}nR\Delta T$$

This equation shows that the work done comes at the expense of the gas's internal energy.

parts.content: If the initial internal energy of the gas is 500 J and it does 200 J of work, determine the final internal energy.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute values into the first law relationship ($U_f - U_i = -W$): $U_f - 500 = -200$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the final internal energy: $300\ \mathrm{J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: State the First Law
steps.content:
To solve this problem, we use the First Law of Thermodynamics, which describes the conservation of energy in a system. The law states that the thermal energy transferred to a system ($Q$) equals the change in internal energy ($\Delta U$) plus the work done *by* the system ($W$):

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

steps.title: Apply adiabatic conditions
steps.content:
The question specifies that the gas undergoes an **adiabatic process** in an **insulated container**. By definition, an adiabatic process has no transfer of thermal energy, so $Q = 0$.

Substituting this into our equation:
$$0 = \Delta U + W$$

steps.title: Determine change in energy
steps.content:
We are given that the gas **does 200 J of work**. Since the work is done *by* the gas (expansion), $W$ is positive ($+200\ \mathrm{J}$).

We can now find the change in internal energy $\Delta U$:
$$\begin{aligned} 0 &= \Delta U + 200 \\ \Delta U &= -200\ \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate final internal energy
steps.content:
The change in internal energy is the difference between the final and initial internal energy: $\Delta U = U_f - U_i$.

We know the **initial internal energy** $U_i = 500\ \mathrm{J}$. Substituting our values:
$$\begin{aligned} U_f - 500 &= -200 \\ U_f &= 500 - 200 \\ U_f &= 300\ \mathrm{J} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Analyze the energy state
steps.content:
First, we identify the initial conditions and the process described. We are told the container is **insulated**, which means no heat enters or leaves the system ($Q = 0$). We are given the **initial internal energy** and the amount of **work** the gas performs on its surroundings.

*   Initial internal energy ($U_{initial}$): $500\ \mathrm{J}$
*   Work done by gas ($W_{out}$): $200\ \mathrm{J}$

highlights.text: initial internal energy of the gas is 500 J

steps.title: Apply conservation of energy
steps.content:
Using the **Conservation of Energy Logic**, we treat the gas's internal energy like a bank balance. Since the container is insulated, no "deposits" (heat) are made. However, the gas "spends" energy to do work.

Therefore, the final internal energy must be the initial energy minus the work done:

$$U_{final} = U_{initial} - \text{Work Done}$$

steps.title: Calculate final internal energy
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the values into our relationship to solve for the final internal energy ($U_{final}$).

$$U_{final} = 500\ \mathrm{J} - 200\ \mathrm{J}$$

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Performing the subtraction gives us the final value.

$$U_{final} = 300\ \mathrm{J}$$

The internal energy has decreased because the gas used its own energy to perform the work.

parts.content: Explain why temperature changes occur in adiabatic processes and how this affects energy storage efficiency.
parts.markscheme:
- **Explain** reason for temperature change: Work is done with no heat transfer ($Q=0$), causing a change in internal energy ($\Delta U = -W$) which changes the temperature. <marks>R1</marks>
- **Explain** effect on efficiency: The temperature change creates a thermal gradient with the surroundings, leading to heat leakage through imperfect insulation, which reduces energy storage efficiency. <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Apply First Law of Thermodynamics
steps.content:
To understand why the temperature changes, we first look at the definition of the process. An **adiabatic process** is defined as one where there is no heat transfer into or out of the system, so $Q = 0$.

Using the First Law of Thermodynamics given by the formula:

$$Q = \Delta U + W$$

Substituting $Q = 0$, we get:

$$0 = \Delta U + W \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta U = -W$$

This tells us that any work done ($W$) by or on the gas comes directly from a change in its internal energy ($\Delta U$).

steps.title: Relate internal energy to temperature
steps.content:
Next, we relate this change in internal energy to temperature. For an ideal gas, the internal energy is determined by the kinetic energy of its molecules. From the data booklet, we see the relationship:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

This shows that internal energy is directly proportional to temperature ($U \propto T$). Therefore, because the work done ($W$) causes a change in internal energy ($\Delta U$), it **must** also cause a change in temperature ($\Delta T$).

steps.title: Explain effect on efficiency
steps.content:
Finally, we consider how this temperature change affects efficiency. When the gas temperature changes, it creates a **thermal gradient** (a temperature difference) between the storage container and the surrounding environment.

In the real world, insulation is never perfect. This temperature difference causes thermal energy to leak through the container walls (heat transfer tries to equalize the temperatures). This leakage represents a loss of stored energy, thereby reducing the overall **energy storage efficiency**.

parts.content: Define the concept of entropy and relate it to the second law of thermodynamics, emphasizing its implications for global warming.
parts.markscheme:

- **State** that the second law of thermodynamics requires the total entropy of an isolated system to always increase over time ($$\Delta S_{\text{total}} \ge 0$$) <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Accept definitions referencing the number of microscopic configurations.</note>

steps.title: Define Entropy
steps.content:
In thermodynamics, **entropy** ($S$) is defined macroscopically as a measure of the **disorder** or **randomness** within a system. 

This concept is covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics**. It describes the tendency of energy to spread out rather than stay concentrated in one place.

highlights.text: measure of the disorder or randomness in a system

steps.title: The Second Law
steps.content:
The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** states that the total entropy of an isolated system (such as the universe) must always increase or remain constant over time. 

Mathematically, this is expressed as:
$$\Delta S_{\text{total}} \ge 0$$

This law implies that natural processes are irreversible and always move toward a state of higher disorder.

highlights.text: total entropy of an isolated system to always increase over time

steps.title: Energy Degradation and Climate
steps.content:
Every time we convert energy (e.g., burning fossil fuels for electricity), the process is not $100\%$ efficient. A portion of the useful energy is always **degraded** into **low-grade thermal energy**, also known as **waste heat**.

In the context of global warming, this waste heat and the byproduct gases from energy production disperse into the environment. While the Greenhouse Effect (Topic B.2) primarily traps solar radiation, the thermodynamic reality is that our industrial energy use constantly increases the total entropy of the Earth-atmosphere system by releasing this thermal energy into our surroundings.

highlights.text: dissipation of useful energy as low-grade thermal energy (waste heat)

steps.title: Define entropy statistically
steps.content:
In the **Statistical Mechanics Approach**, entropy is defined based on the number of microscopic configurations (or **microstates**) that a system can occupy. 

Mathematically, a system with a higher number of microstates corresponds to a higher level of disorder or randomness. When we say a system has high entropy, we mean there are many ways for the energy and particles to be arranged while maintaining the same macroscopic appearance.

highlights.text: Define entropy as a measure of the disorder or randomness in a system

steps.title: State the Second Law
steps.content:
The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** states that the total entropy of an isolated system (such as the universe) must always increase or remain constant over time. 

$$\Delta S_{\text{total}} \ge 0$$

From a statistical perspective, this happens because systems naturally evolve toward states that have the highest probability—which are the states with the most possible microscopic configurations (maximum disorder).

highlights.text: the second law of thermodynamics requires the total entropy of an isolated system to always increase over time ($$\Delta S_{\text{total}} \ge 0$$)

steps.title: Implications for Global Warming
steps.content:
Whenever we perform an energy conversion (like burning fuel in an engine), the Second Law dictates that we cannot convert all energy into useful work. 

Some energy is inevitably dissipated as **low-grade thermal energy** (waste heat). This dispersal of energy increases the entropy of the environment. In the context of global warming, our industrial processes continuously release this waste heat and greenhouse gases into the atmosphere, leading to an overall increase in the thermal energy stored in the Earth's system.

highlights.text: energy processes result in the dissipation of useful energy as low-grade thermal energy (waste heat) which disperses into the environment

parts.content: Discuss how the entropy of an isolated system changes over time and its impact on environmental systems.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that the entropy of an isolated system not at equilibrium will continually increase until it reaches a maximum <marks>A1</marks>
- **Discuss** the impact on environmental systems, noting that increased entropy implies greater disorder (e.g., mixing of pollutants, degradation of materials, loss of useful energy) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: The Second Law of Thermodynamics
steps.content:
In thermodynamics, we focus on the macroscopic behavior of systems. The **Second Law of Thermodynamics** states that the total entropy of an **isolated system** that is not in equilibrium will naturally increase over time. 

This means that processes in nature have a specific direction—they move toward states of higher disorder.

steps.title: Reaching Maximum Entropy
steps.content:
As the system evolves, entropy continues to rise until it reaches a **maximum**. At this point, the system is in **thermodynamic equilibrium**. 

Once a system reaches maximum entropy, it can no longer undergo spontaneous changes because there are no longer any temperature or pressure gradients available to do work. This is the first key point for your marks.

highlights.text: entropy of an isolated system not at equilibrium will continually increase until it reaches a maximum

steps.title: Impact on Environmental Systems
steps.content:
From a macroscopic perspective, an increase in entropy in our environment results in the **degradation of energy**. High-quality, "ordered" energy (like chemical energy in fuels) is converted into low-quality, "disordered" thermal energy (heat) that is less useful for doing work.

In environmental systems, this manifests as:
1. **Mixing of pollutants:** Pollutants naturally spread out and mix into the air or water (increasing disorder), making them harder to remove.
2. **Material degradation:** Buildings and natural structures break down over time.
3. **Loss of useful energy:** Energy transfers in the biosphere always result in some energy being "lost" as waste heat to the surroundings.

steps.title: Summary of Marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks for this question:
*   **Mark 1:** State that entropy in an isolated system increases until it hits a maximum at equilibrium.
*   **Mark 2:** Discuss how this leads to increased disorder in the environment, such as the degradation of energy or the spreading of pollutants.

steps.title: Understanding the Statistical Approach
steps.content:
In the **Statistical/Disorder Approach**, we view entropy as a measure of the number of possible ways a system can be arranged (microstates). 

Systems naturally move from "ordered" states (fewer microstates) to "disordered" states (many more microstates) simply because the disordered states are statistically much more likely to occur. This transition represents an increase in entropy.

steps.title: Evolution of Isolated Systems
steps.content:
For an isolated system that is not yet at equilibrium, the Second Law of Thermodynamics states that its entropy will continually increase over time. This process continues until the system reaches a state of maximum disorder, known as **equilibrium**.

At this point, the entropy is at its maximum possible value, and no further spontaneous changes in the system's macroscopic state will occur.

highlights.text: entropy of an isolated system not at equilibrium will continually increase until it reaches a maximum

steps.title: Impact on Environmental Systems
steps.content:
In environmental systems, this natural tendency toward disorder has several critical impacts:

1.  **Mixing of Pollutants:** A concentrated source of pollution is an "ordered" state. Over time, pollutants naturally spread out and mix with the air or water because a dispersed state has a higher number of microstates (higher entropy).
2.  **Material Degradation:** Solid structures or complex molecules naturally break down into simpler, more scattered components over time.
3.  **Energy Degradation:** Useful, concentrated energy (like chemical energy in fuel) naturally transforms into disordered thermal energy (heat) that is dissipated into the surroundings and becomes less available to do work.

highlights.text: increased entropy implies greater disorder (e.g., mixing of pollutants, degradation of materials, loss of useful energy)

steps.title: Define cosmological heat death
steps.content:
To understand the long-term evolution of the universe, we look at the Second Law of Thermodynamics. This law states that the total entropy of an isolated system (like the universe) must always increase over time. 

Eventually, the universe is predicted to reach a state of **maximum entropy** known as "heat death." In this state, energy is uniformly distributed across space. Because there are no longer any temperature or pressure gradients, no work can be extracted to power stars, galaxies, or life. This earns the first mark.

highlights.text: heat death" refers to the universe reaching a state of maximum entropy where energy is uniformly distributed and no work can be extracted

steps.title: Link to environmental challenges
steps.content:
This cosmological concept directly mirrors our current environmental challenges through the lens of **energy degradation**. While the First Law of Thermodynamics tells us energy is conserved, the Second Law tells us that every time we use energy, we change it from a "useful" low-entropy form (like chemical energy in fuels) into a "non-useful" high-entropy form (like waste heat dissipated into the atmosphere).

Because this process is **irreversible**, our consumption of resources leads to a permanent reduction in the total amount of useful energy available to humanity. This highlights the urgency of sustainability and forms the basis for the second mark.

highlights.text: consumption of energy resources is irreversible, leading to a permanent reduction in available useful energy

parts.content: Calculate the work done by the gas during the expansion.
parts.markscheme:
- **Substitute** correct unit conversions into work formula: $P = 2 \times 1.013 \times 10^5 = 2.026 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ AND $\Delta V = 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$ <marks>M1</marks>

- **Calculate** the final value: $W = 2.026 \times 10^5 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \approx 405 \text{ J}$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept answers in the range 400 J to 410 J. Award full marks for correct answer in J or kJ. Award M1 for calculating 4 atm L without conversion.</note>

steps.title: Identify the Work formula
steps.content:
To calculate the work done by a gas expanding at a constant pressure, we use the thermodynamic work formula:

$$W = PΔV$$

Where:
- $W$ is the work done (in Joules, J)
- $P$ is the constant external pressure (in Pascals, Pa)
- $ΔV$ is the change in volume (in cubic meters, m$^3$)

This concept is covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics**. Before substituting values, we must ensure all units are converted to the SI system.

steps.title: Convert Pressure to Pascals
steps.content:
The pressure is given in atmospheres (atm). To convert this to the SI unit of Pascals (Pa), we use the standard conversion factor: $1 \text{ atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$.

$$\begin{aligned} P &= 2 \text{ atm} \times (1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa/atm}) \\ P &= 2.026 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \end{aligned}$$

This conversion is a critical first step to ensuring our final answer is in Joules.

steps.title: Calculate Volume Change in m³
steps.content:
First, find the change in volume ($\Delta V$) in Liters (L):
$$\Delta V = V_{\text{final}} - V_{\text{initial}} = 3 \text{ L} - 1 \text{ L} = 2 \text{ L}$$

Next, convert Liters to the SI unit of cubic meters (m$^3$). Since $1 \text{ m}^3 = 1000 \text{ L}$:
$$\begin{aligned} \Delta V &= 2 \text{ L} \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{L} \\ \Delta V &= 2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the final work
steps.content:
Now, substitute the SI values into the work formula:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= P\Delta V \\ W &= (2.026 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}) \times (2 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3) \\ W &= 405.2 \text{ J} \end{aligned}$$

Rounding to three significant figures, we get approximately $405 \text{ J}$. According to the markscheme, you earn one mark (**M1**) for the correct unit conversions and one mark (**A1**) for the final calculation.

steps.title: Identify given variables
steps.content:
To calculate the work done by the gas, we first need to identify the initial state and the final state of the system from the problem description. 

We are given:
- External pressure ($P$): $2 \text{ atm}$
- Initial volume ($V_1$): $1 \text{ L}$
- Final volume ($V_2$): $3 \text{ L}$

steps.title: Calculate change in volume
steps.content:
In thermodynamics, work done at a constant pressure is related to the change in volume ($\Delta V$). We calculate this by subtracting the initial volume from the final volume:

$$\Delta V = V_2 - V_1$$
$$\Delta V = 3 \text{ L} - 1 \text{ L} = 2 \text{ L}$$

steps.title: Calculate work in atm·L
steps.content:
Using the Litre-Atmosphere approach, we first find the work done in units of atmosphere-litres ($\text{atm}\cdot\text{L}$). The formula for work done by a gas at constant pressure is:

$$W = P \Delta V$$

Substituting our values:
$$W = 2 \text{ atm} \times 2 \text{ L} = 4 \text{ atm}\cdot\text{L}$$

This is the work done expressed in non-SI units, which earns the first mark (M1) according to the markscheme.

steps.title: Convert work to Joules
steps.content:
To find the answer in Joules (J), we use the conversion factor between $\text{atm}\cdot\text{L}$ and Joules. 

Since $1 \text{ atm} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$ and $1 \text{ L} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$, the conversion factor is:
$$1 \text{ atm}\cdot\text{L} \approx 101.3 \text{ J}$$

Now, convert our result:
$$W = 4 \text{ atm}\cdot\text{L} \times 101.3 \text{ J/atm}\cdot\text{L} = 405.2 \text{ J}$$

steps.content:
Rounding to three significant figures as required by typical IB standards, we get:

$$W \approx 405 \text{ J}$$

This matches the final value required for the second mark (A1).

steps.title: Relate expansion to energy
steps.content:
To determine the direction of energy transfer, we look at the thermodynamic definition of work:

$$W = P\Delta V$$

Because the gas **expands** (moves from $1\text{ L}$ to $3\text{ L}$), the change in volume $\Delta V$ is positive. This indicates that the system is doing work on its surroundings. In terms of energy, this means that **energy is being transferred out of the gas system** and into the environment.

steps.title: Summary of energy changes
steps.content:
To earn full marks, your explanation must include:
1.  The identification that work is the **area under the P-V curve**.
2.  The statement that because the gas expands, energy is **transferred from the gas to the surroundings**.

This transfer reduces the energy stored within the gas (unless heat is added simultaneously to compensate).

steps.title: Interpret work as area
steps.content:
In thermodynamics, when a gas undergoes a process, the work done ($W$) can be visualized on a Pressure-Volume ($P$-$V$) diagram. 

Graphically, the work done is equivalent to the **area under the curve** that describes the process. For a constant pressure process, this area forms a rectangle with a height equal to the pressure and a width equal to the change in volume.

$$W = \text{Area under } P\text{-}V \text{ curve}$$

steps.title: Relate work to energy transfer
steps.content:
The question asks how this relates to energy changes. Since the gas **expands**, it must push against the external environment to increase its volume. 

Doing this requires the gas to exert a force over a distance, meaning the gas performs work on its surroundings. Consequently, this work represents the magnitude of **energy transferred from the gas to the surroundings**. This results in a decrease in the system's energy (unless balanced by heat input), as defined by the First Law of Thermodynamics.

$$W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV$$

highlights.text: Explain how this work relates to the energy changes in the system.

parts.content: Discuss how the process would differ if the expansion were adiabatic, particularly in terms of energy loss.
parts.markscheme:
- **State** that an adiabatic expansion involves no heat transfer ($Q = 0$) <marks>A1</marks>

- **Explain** that the work is done at the expense of the internal energy of the gas, leading to a decrease in temperature <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award A1 for stating condition of no heat transfer. Award A1 for linking work done to decrease in internal energy.</note>

steps.title: Define the adiabatic condition
steps.content:
An **adiabatic** process is one in which no thermal energy (heat) is transferred into or out of the system. This is a fundamental concept in **Topic B.4 (Thermodynamics)**.

$$Q = 0$$

steps.title: Apply First Law equation
steps.content:
We use the First Law of Thermodynamics, which relates internal energy change ($\Delta U$), heat ($Q$), and work done by the system ($W$):

$$\Delta U = Q - W$$

Substituting the adiabatic condition ($Q = 0$) into the equation gives us:

$$\Delta U = -W$$

steps.title: Analyze energy and temperature
steps.content:
Since the gas expands, it is performing work on the surroundings, meaning $W$ is positive. Based on the equation $\Delta U = -W$, the internal energy must decrease.

$$\Delta U < 0$$

A decrease in internal energy directly results in a **decrease in the temperature** of the gas. Unlike the constant pressure process, no external energy (heat) enters to replace the energy lost to work.

steps.title: Define adiabatic process
steps.content:
In physics, an **adiabatic** process is one where no thermal energy (heat) is transferred into or out of the system. This means that for this expansion:

$$Q = 0$$

This is a key starting point for understanding how the energy balance changes compared to other processes.

steps.title: Analyze molecular collisions
steps.content:
From a **kinetic theory** perspective, we look at what happens to individual gas molecules. As the gas expands, the boundary (like a piston) is moving outwards.

When gas molecules collide with this retreating boundary, they rebound with a lower speed than they had before the collision. This is because they do work on the boundary to move it forward.

steps.title: Link kinetic energy to temperature
steps.content:
Since the molecules lose speed during these collisions, their average kinetic energy ($\overline{E_k}$) decreases. According to the data booklet, the temperature of a gas is directly proportional to the average random kinetic energy of its molecules:

$$\overline{E_{k}}=\frac{3}{2}k_{B}T$$

Because the molecules are losing kinetic energy to do work and no heat ($Q$) is entering the system to replace it, the temperature ($T$) of the gas must drop.

steps.title: Summarize energy loss
steps.content: In summary, in an adiabatic expansion, the work done by the gas comes entirely at the expense of its **internal energy**. Unlike a constant pressure expansion where heat might be added to maintain temperature, the adiabatic process results in a significant **decrease in temperature** and internal energy because of the lack of heat transfer.

parts.content: Discuss why entropy increases in irreversible processes and its implications for sustainability.
parts.markscheme:
### Entropy Increase
- State that entropy is a measure of disorder/randomness. <marks>M1</marks>
- Explain that in irreversible processes, energy is dissipated/dispersed, which always leads to an increase in disorder. <marks>A1</marks>

### Implications for Sustainability
- Link increased entropy to sustainability: Implies resources are being used inefficiently or wasted (energy degradation). <marks>M1</marks>
- Conclude on the impact: Leads to increased pollution/waste and systems becoming less ordered/efficient over time, eventually causing environmental degradation. <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Define entropy
steps.content:
To understand why entropy changes, we first need a clear definition. In the context of **Topic B.4 (Thermodynamics)**, entropy is defined as a measure of the disorder or randomness within a system. 

When a system becomes more chaotic or its energy becomes more spread out, its entropy increases.

steps.title: Explain energy degradation
steps.content:
Using the **Thermodynamic Energy Focus**, we look at the quality of energy. In any irreversible process (like a car engine burning fuel or friction slowing down a wheel), high-quality, organized energy is dissipated or dispersed into the surroundings. 

This process converts useful energy into low-quality thermal energy. Because this thermal energy is randomly distributed, it leads to an overall increase in the disorder of the universe. This is a core part of the Second Law of Thermodynamics:

$$\Delta S_{total} > 0$$

steps.title: Link to resource efficiency
steps.content:
The increase in entropy has a direct impact on sustainability. High entropy implies that energy has been degraded to a state where it can no longer perform useful work. 

From a sustainability standpoint, this means resources are being used **inefficiently**. Every time we increase entropy unnecessarily, we are effectively 'wasting' the concentrated energy sources that future generations might need.

steps.title: Evaluate environmental impact
steps.content:
Finally, the continuous increase of entropy in industrial and environmental systems leads to long-term degradation. As energy is degraded into heat and materials are scattered, we see:

*   **Increased pollution:** Waste products represent high-entropy states of matter.
*   **System decline:** Environmental systems become less ordered and less efficient over time.

To be truly sustainable, we must aim to minimize the rate of entropy production by improving efficiency and reducing waste.

steps.title: Define entropy as disorder
steps.content:
To understand why entropy increases, we first define it. In thermodynamics, **entropy ($S$)** is a macroscopic property that serves as a measure of the **disorder** or **randomness** within a system.

From a systems perspective, a highly ordered state (like a structured fuel source) has low entropy, while a dispersed, chaotic state (like exhaust gases) has high entropy.

steps.title: Explain irreversible energy dispersion
steps.content:
In any **irreversible process**—which includes almost all real-world environmental processes—energy is not lost (due to the First Law of Thermodynamics), but it is **dissipated** or dispersed. 

For example, when energy is converted from one form to another, some is always "degraded" into thermal energy (heat) that spreads out into the environment. This spreading of energy inevitably leads to an increase in the total disorder of the universe, meaning the total entropy must increase ($ ΔS > 0 $).

highlights.text: energy is dissipated/dispersed, which always leads to an increase in disorder

steps.title: Link entropy to sustainability
steps.content:
The increase in entropy has profound implications for **sustainability**. When we use resources in irreversible ways (like burning fossil fuels), we are converting low-entropy, concentrated energy into high-entropy, dispersed energy.

This process is known as **energy degradation**. Because this "disordered" energy is less available to do useful work, it implies that resources are being used inefficiently. A sustainable system aims to minimize this rate of entropy production to preserve the quality of available energy.

highlights.text: Implies resources are being used inefficiently or wasted (energy degradation)

steps.title: Analyze environmental degradation
steps.content:
Finally, the "disorder" associated with increased entropy manifests physically as **waste and pollution**. 

In environmental systems, high-entropy outputs (such as chemical pollutants, carbon dioxide, or heat islands) represent a breakdown of the organized structures of the ecosystem. As entropy accumulates, the system becomes less efficient and more chaotic, eventually leading to **environmental degradation** and a loss of the system's ability to support life or provide services.

highlights.text: Leads to increased pollution/waste and systems becoming less ordered/efficient over time

steps.title: Define entropy
steps.content:
To start, we need to define what we mean by entropy. In thermodynamics, **entropy** is a physical property that serves as a measure of the **disorder or randomness** within a system.

From an environmental perspective, think of low entropy as "ordered" energy (like a battery or a concentrated fuel source) and high entropy as "disordered" energy (like heat dissipating into the atmosphere).

steps.title: Explain energy dispersal
steps.content:
In the real world, most processes are **irreversible**. When these processes occur, energy is not destroyed (following the Law of Conservation of Energy), but it is **dispersed** or spread out. 

This dispersal always leads to an increase in total disorder. Because the energy becomes more spread out, the entropy of the system and its surroundings increases. This concept is covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics**.

steps.title: Link to work potential
steps.content:
Using the **Efficiency and Work Potential** approach, we can see that as entropy increases, the energy becomes "degraded." 

High-quality energy (low entropy) has a high potential to do **useful work** (like moving a car). Once that energy is converted into heat and dispersed (high entropy), it loses its potential to do work. This means that every irreversible process results in a permanent loss of available energy, making the system less efficient overall.

steps.title: Connect to sustainability
steps.content:
This has major implications for **sustainability**. If a system has high entropy production, it means resources are being used **inefficiently** and energy is being wasted as heat. 

Sustainability relies on maintaining the "work potential" of our resources. Rapid entropy increase implies we are quickly converting high-quality resources into low-quality waste, which is the definition of an unsustainable practice.

steps.title: Identify environmental impact
steps.content:
Finally, the accumulation of entropy leads to **environmental degradation**. 

As systems become less ordered and more energy is dispersed as waste, we see an increase in **pollution** and a breakdown of the complex, ordered structures within ecosystems. Over time, this makes the environment less capable of supporting life and regenerating resources, directly threatening long-term sustainability.

questions.specification: An ideal gas in a cylinder is compressed isothermally from $4.00\ \mathrm{L}$ to $1.00\ \mathrm{L}$. If the amount of gas is $n$ moles, what is the change in entropy of the gas?

parts.content:
The $P-V$ diagram illustrates a cycle $A \to B \to C \to A$ performed by an ideal diatomic gas.

![diagram](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/question-images/ramen-ramen-ib-physics-new-bef369af-96cc-49ed-90da-39b12b7e6dde-1771929956947/108abbfc-25c4-4ee6-b505-621eb7853ede.jpeg)

Process $A \to B$ is an isobaric expansion at a pressure of $2.5 \times 10^5$ Pa.

Calculate the work done by the gas during process $A \to B$.
parts.markscheme:
- $W = P\Delta V$  
- $W = (2.5 \times 10^5) \times (5.0 - 2.0) \times 10^{-3} = 750$ J  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: In process $B \to C$, thermal energy is removed from the gas. With reference to the second law of thermodynamics, explain why a refrigerator (heat pump) performing a reverse cycle requires an external work input to transfer thermal energy from a cold reservoir to a hot reservoir.
parts.markscheme:
- State the Clausius version of the second law: thermal energy cannot spontaneously flow from a region of lower temperature to a region of higher temperature  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Explain that without work input, the entropy of the universe would decrease, which violates the second law  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Work input is required to ensure that more heat is rejected to the hot reservoir than is taken from the cold reservoir, allowing the total entropy change to be $\geq 0$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Process $C \to A$ is an adiabatic compression where $PV^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$. Show that for an ideal gas, the pressure $P$ and temperature $T$ during this process satisfy the relationship $P^{1-\gamma}T^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Start with the ideal gas law $PV = nRT$ and rearrange for volume $V = \frac{nRT}{P}$  
- Substitute this into the adiabatic equation: $P\left(\frac{nRT}{P}\right)^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Simplify to show $P^{1-\gamma} T^{\gamma} = \text{constant}$ (assuming $n, R$ are constants)  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
For this diatomic gas, the ratio of specific heat capacities is $\gamma = 1.4$. The temperature of the gas at state $C$ is $290$ K.

Calculate the temperature of the gas at state $A$.
parts.markscheme:
- Use the adiabatic relationship between $T$ and $V$: $T_A V_A^{\gamma-1} = T_C V_C^{\gamma-1}$ 
- $T_A = 290 \times \left(\frac{5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2.0 \times 10^{-3}}\right)^{1.4-1} = 290 \times 2.5^{0.4}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>
- $T_A = 418$ K (or 420 K) <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
During a different isothermal compression occurring at a constant temperature of $300$ K, $660$ J of thermal energy is rejected to a large thermal reservoir.

Calculate the change in entropy of the reservoir.
parts.markscheme:
- Use $\Delta S = \frac{Q}{T} = \frac{660}{300}$  
- $\Delta S = 2.2$ J K$^{-1}$  <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the theoretical efficiency of this engine.
parts.markscheme:
- State the efficiency formula: $\eta = 1 - \frac{T_\mathrm{c}}{T_\mathrm{h}}$
- Substitute values: $\eta = 1 - \frac{300}{500}$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Calculate the theoretical efficiency: $\eta = 0.4$ (or $40\%$) <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the temperatures
steps.content:
First, we need to identify the operating temperatures of the engine from the question. For the Carnot efficiency formula, these temperatures must be in Kelvin.

The high temperature ($T_h$) and low temperature ($T_c$) are given as:
- $T_h = 500 \text{ K}$
- $T_c = 300 \text{ K}$

steps.title: Apply Carnot efficiency formula
steps.content:
The theoretical efficiency of a Carnot engine is determined entirely by the temperatures of the hot and cold reservoirs. This concept is covered in **Topic B.4: Thermodynamics (HL only)**.

We use the Carnot efficiency formula:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{T_c}{T_h}$$

steps.title: Substitute values
steps.content:
Substitute the values of $T_c = 300$ and $T_h = 500$ into the equation:

$$\eta = 1 - \frac{300}{500}$$

steps.title: Calculate the result
steps.content:
Now, compute the final value. First, divide the temperatures:

$$\frac{300}{500} = 0.6$$

Then subtract from 1:

$$\eta = 1 - 0.6 = 0.4$$

We can express this as a percentage by multiplying by 100:

$$\text{Efficiency} = 40\%$$

parts.content: Discuss the factors that prevent real engines from achieving Carnot efficiency, particularly in renewable technologies.
parts.markscheme:
- Irreversible processes/energy losses occur in real engines due to:
- State friction or viscous effects <marks>A1</marks>
- State heat transfer across finite temperature differences <marks>A1</marks>
- Incomplete combustion / chemical reactions
- Pressure drops in fluid flow
- Mechanical / electrical energy losses

2 marks total

<note>Award marks for any two valid factors.</note>

<examTip>Mention that renewable energy technologies like solar thermal plants, geothermal power plants etc. also suffer from these irreversibilities, preventing them from achieving Carnot efficiency.</examTip>

steps.title: Ideal vs. Real Engines
steps.content: The Carnot engine represents a theoretical maximum efficiency based on a perfectly **reversible** cycle. Real engines, however, always involve **irreversible processes** that cause energy to be lost to the surroundings rather than converted into useful work. This question asks us to identify the specific physical causes of these losses.

steps.title: Mechanical Irreversibility
steps.content: The first major factor is energy loss due to mechanical interactions. In any real machine with moving parts (like the turbines in a geothermal plant), **mechanical friction** dissipates kinetic energy as heat. Additionally, the working fluid experiences internal resistance known as **viscous effects** as it flows through pipes and valves.

steps.title: Thermal Irreversibility
steps.content: The second factor relates to how heat is moved. The ideal Carnot cycle assumes heat transfer happens isothermally (with zero temperature difference), which would take infinite time. Real engines require a **finite temperature difference** to transfer heat quickly. Heat flowing across a temperature difference is an irreversible process that increases entropy and reduces efficiency compared to the Carnot limit.

highlights.text: State heat transfer across finite temperature differences

steps.title: Renewable Context
steps.content: In the context of renewable energy mentioned in the question, these irreversibilities appear as fluid resistance in geothermal pipes, heat losses to the environment from solar thermal collectors, or friction in generator bearings. These practical limitations prevent the system from reaching the theoretical Carnot efficiency.

steps.title: Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To secure the 2 marks, you simply need to state two distinct sources of irreversibility. The standard answers expected by the markscheme are:
1. Energy losses due to **friction** or **viscous effects**.
2. Energy losses due to **heat transfer across finite temperature differences**.
